vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Acetylsalicylic acid (ASA, Aspirin; CAS Nº 50-78-2)  
Scenario: Daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) for 28 days to an adult human.  
Target system considered: Digestive tract in the broad sense (oral cavity → oesophagus → stomach → small & large bowel → accessory organs: liver, pancreas, gall bladder).  
Approach: Weight-of-evidence integrating human clinical experience, controlled trials, animal 28-day studies (OECD 407 type) and mechanistic data. Key sources: FDA OTC monograph, ECHA IUCLID summary, PubMed clinical studies, Goodman & Gilman, EMA assessment reports.

1. Definition of an MTD for humans  
• Therapeutic high-dose regimens in adults with rheumatic disorders are 3–4 g/day (≈45–60 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹).  
• Dose-limiting toxicity in such regimens is almost always gastrointestinal.  
Consequently, 3–4 g/day is considered a pragmatic human MTD for a 28-day period.

2. Observed / predicted digestive-system findings

A. Upper gastrointestinal tract (stomach, duodenum)  
Functional effects  
• Epigastric pain, heartburn, nausea, vomiting, dyspepsia (incidence 15–40 % at ≥3 g/day; Derry 2000, Lanas 2011).  
• Occult or overt upper-GI bleeding, haematemesis, melena; fall in Hb/Hct ≥2 g dL⁻¹ in ~3 % of chronic high-dose users (Silverstein 2000).  
Histopathology/endoscopy  
• Acute erosive gastritis, petechiae, sub-mucosal haemorrhages after only a few doses; progression to linear erosions and gastric/duodenal ulcers within 7–14 days in 10–25 % of subjects at MTD (Bjarnason 2018).  
Clinical pathology  
• Positive faecal occult blood, iron-deficiency anaemia, ↑ blood urea/creatinine ratio (digestion of blood proteins).  
Mechanism  
1. Topical irritation: ASA is a weak acid (pKa 3.5). In the low-pH gastric lumen it remains unionised, penetrates epithelial cells, ionises in the neutral cytosol (“ion trapping”) → intracellular acidity & injury.  
2. Systemic inhibition of COX-1 → ↓ mucosal prostaglandins (PGE₂, PGI₂) → reduced mucus & bicarbonate secretion, reduced mucosal blood flow, delayed restitution → loss of barrier → acid/pepsin injury.  
3. Platelet COX-1 inhibition (life-span effect) → impaired haemostasis → facilitates bleeding of any mucosal lesion.  
Adversity / adaptiveness  
• Clearly adverse.  
Reversibility  
• Yes, the gastric epithelium regenerates within 3–7 days once ASA is stopped; ulcers heal within 4–6 weeks ± acid suppression.  
Monitorability in clinic  
• Symptom review, faecal occult blood test (FOBT), Hb/Hct, serum ferritin; endoscopy when indicated.

B. Small and large intestine  
Functional effects  
• Abdominal discomfort, occult lower-GI bleeding; less frequently overt haemorrhage.  
Histopathology / imaging  
• Increased intestinal permeability (Cr-EDTA test), focal erosions, diaphragm-like strictures after prolonged NSAID use (Langman 1999; animal data support diaphragms after 4–6 weeks at high doses).  
Mechanism  
• Same systemic COX-1 blockade-mediated prostaglandin deficit; plus enterohepatic recirculation of salicylate exposing mucosa repeatedly.  
Adversity / reversibility  
• Erosions/bleeding = adverse; generally reversible. Chronic strictures are rare and can be irreversible.  
Monitoring  
• FOBT, capsule endoscopy, haemoglobin, C-reactive protein.

C. Hepatobiliary system  
Functional / clinical pathology effects  
• Dose-related, usually asymptomatic ↑ ALT/AST (≤3× ULN) in 1–4 % of adults at ≥3 g/day (Larrey 2015).  
• Very rare severe hepatic injury; microvesicular steatosis & Reye-like syndrome mainly in children.  
Histopathology (animal & rare biopsy data)  
• Centrilobular microvesicular steatosis, mild hepatocellular necrosis at plasma salicylate >300 µg mL⁻¹.  
Mechanism  
• Mitochondrial uncoupling → ↓ ATP, impaired β-oxidation → steatosis; oxidative stress.  
Adversity  
• ALT/AST ≤3× ULN usually viewed as adaptive / non-adverse; necrosis is adverse.  
Reversibility  
• Transaminase elevations resolve within 1–2 weeks after cessation.  
Monitoring  
• Baseline and weekly ALT, AST, bilirubin; clinical jaundice assessment.

D. Pancreas  
• Isolated case-reports of pancreatitis; incidence too low to attribute confidently—no signal in 28-day studies. Not expected at MTD with current evidence.

E. Oral cavity / oesophagus  
• Direct mucosal irritation rare because tablets disintegrate in stomach; enteric-coated products avoid oesophageal contact. No consistent lesions reported.

3. Integrated toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability

Systemic availability (oral, 3–4 g/day) = High  
• Rapid absorption; 68–75 % bioavailability of salicylate; peak plasma 200–400 µg mL⁻¹ at MTD.  
Toxicodynamic activity (digestive tract) = High for gastric/duodenal mucosa, Medium for intestine, Low-to-Medium for liver.

4. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Most controlled human studies are ≤14 days; 28-day data rely partly on observational cohorts and read-across from other non-selective NSAIDs.  
• Individual susceptibility factors (H. pylori, alcohol use, genetics) not fully quantifiable.

5. Summary table of effects

1. Gastric/duodenal erosions & ulcers  
   – Adverse, reversible, clinically monitorable (symptoms, FOBT, endoscopy).

2. Upper-GI bleeding / anaemia  
   – Adverse, reversible (if controlled), readily monitorable (Hb/Hct).

3. Intestinal erosions / permeability increase  
   – Adverse, usually reversible; monitorable (FOBT, capsule scope).

4. Diaphragm-like strictures (rare)  
   – Adverse, potentially irreversible; detectable by imaging/endoscopy.

5. Mild transaminase elevation  
   – Usually adaptive/non-adverse; reversible; monitorable (liver panel).

6. Hepatocellular necrosis (very rare)  
   – Adverse, reversible in most cases; monitorable (liver panel, symptoms).

Overall conclusion  
At a true maximum-tolerated daily dose for 28 days, Aspirin poses a high probability of adverse, but largely reversible, gastrointestinal toxicity driven by topical acidity, systemic COX-1 inhibition and impaired haemostasis. Regular clinical monitoring (symptoms, FOBT, CBC, liver enzymes) allows early detection and management.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Paracetamol (acetaminophen; CAS 103-90-2)  
Scenario: Adult human, oral administration, “maximum tolerated dose (MTD)” once daily for 28 consecutive days.  

NOTE ON THE DOSE SETTING  
•  In controlled clinical trials, 4 g/day is generally regarded as the highest dose that can be administered for several weeks without an unacceptable incidence of serious hepatic injury; it will therefore be used as the pragmatic MTD in this assessment.  
•  A single dose ≥7.5–10 g (≈150 mg/kg) is the usual human threshold for acute hepatic failure (Rumack, 2004). Repeating 4 g/day for ≥14 days already produces biochemical liver effects in a sizable minority of subjects (Watkins et al., 2006; ECHA registration dossier, 2023).

1.  ADME CONSIDERATIONS  
•  Absorption: Rapid and almost complete after oral intake (F ≈ 0.80-0.90).  
•  Distribution: Low plasma-protein binding (~25 %), Vd ≈ 0.9 L/kg.  
•  Metabolism: 90-95 % undergoes hepatic phase-II conjugation (glucuronidation > sulfation). 5-10 % is oxidised by CYP2E1, 1A2 and 3A4 to the reactive electrophile N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI).  
•  Excretion: Conjugates are eliminated renally, t½ ≈ 2 h in healthy adults.  
Systemic availability: HIGH.  
2.  WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE FOR DIGESTIVE-SYSTEM TOXICITY AFTER 28-DAY MTD  

2.1  Functional / clinical effects  
a) Upper gastrointestinal tract  
   • Nausea, vomiting, dyspepsia and epigastric pain are reported in 2-5 % of subjects at 4 g/day (FDA, 2016; Prescott, 1983).  
   • Unlike NSAIDs, paracetamol does not inhibit COX-1 and rarely causes endoscopic mucosal lesions (Laine et al., 2008).  
   • Stool occult blood tests remain negative in most repeat-dose studies.  
b) Hepatobiliary system (part of the digestive system)  
   • 14-28 days at 4 g/day: 31-44 % of healthy adults show serum ALT >3 × ULN between days 7 and 21; some also have AST and total bilirubin increases (Watkins et al., 2006; Larson et al., 2012).  
   • Clinical symptoms are usually absent, but mild right-upper-quadrant tenderness, anorexia or fatigue may develop.  
   • A small minority (<1 %) can progress to overt hepatotoxicity (jaundice, coagulopathy, hypoglycaemia) if dosing continues (Lee, 2017).  
c) Lower gastrointestinal tract and pancreas  
   • Diarrhoea or constipation occur sporadically and are not dose-related; mechanistic linkage is weak.  
   • Case-control data indicate a very low incidence of pancreatitis and are confounded by ethanol use; no consistent signal in 28-day studies.  

2.2  Histopathology / clinical pathology  
a) Stomach & intestine: generally unremarkable. Rare reports of mild gastritis without ulceration.  
b) Liver (predominant target organ)  
   • Light microscopy: Centrilobular (zone 3) hepatocyte swelling → cytolytic necrosis → bridging necrosis if exposure continues (Makin et al., 2001).  
   • Electron microscopy: Mitochondrial swelling, loss of cristae, and nitrosative stress markers.  
   • Clinical pathology:  
        – ALT/AST elevation (early signal, often >1 000 IU/L in severe cases).  
        – ↑ INR/PT, ↑ total bilirubin, ↓ glucose in progressive injury.  
   • Plasma glutathione disulphide and acylcarnitine profiles change within 24 h and are emerging biomarkers (McGill et al., 2014).  

3.  MECHANISTIC EXPLANATION  

Step 1 – Bioactivation: At therapeutic doses conjugation is dominant. Daily 4 g saturates sulfation in some individuals (especially with fasting or chronic ethanol use) and shifts more substrate to CYP2E1/1A2/3A4.  

Step 2 – Reactive intermediate: NAPQI is a strong electrophile that covalently binds cysteinyl residues on mitochondrial and cytosolic proteins once intracellular glutathione (GSH) falls below ≈30 % of normal.  

Step 3 – Oxidative & nitrosative stress: Protein adducts impair complex I/II of the respiratory chain, generating ROS and peroxynitrite.  

Step 4 – Mitochondrial permeability transition (MPT) and ATP collapse lead to oncotic necrosis of centrilobular hepatocytes.  

Step 5 – Sterile inflammation mediated by DAMPs (e.g., HMGB1) amplifies injury.  

Gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea, vomiting, epigastric pain) are largely secondary to:  
• Direct central chemoreceptor trigger zone stimulation by paracetamol or metabolites.  
• Hepatic capsular distension and cytokine release during early hepatocellular injury.  

4.  CHARACTERISATION OF EACH EFFECT  

1) Nausea / vomiting / dyspepsia  
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (no structural necrosis)  
   Adverse or adaptive: Usually ADAPTIVE (transient, protective expulsion reflex)  
   Reversibility: RAPID (hours-days after dose reduction/cessation)  
   Monitorable: Yes – patient-reported outcomes, anti-emetic use  

2) Mild, transient ALT/AST elevation (<3×ULN)  
   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
   Nature: ADAPTIVE/REGENERATIVE (reflects enzyme leakage during turnover)  
   Reversibility: Yes, within 3-7 days after stopping drug  
   Monitorable: Yes – routine liver‐function tests  

3) Marked ALT/AST elevation (≥3×ULN) ± hyper‐bilirubinaemia  
   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
   Nature: ADVERSE – indicates true hepatocellular injury  
   Reversibility: Usually reversible if recognised early and dosing stopped; N-acetylcysteine (NAC) can accelerate recovery. Irreversible in rare cases progressing to acute liver failure.  
   Monitorable: Yes – LFTs, INR, bilirubin, clinical assessment  

4) Histological centrilobular necrosis  
   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
   Nature: ADVERSE  
   Reversibility: Potentially reversible through hepatic regeneration if <50 % of hepatocyte mass lost; irreversible if massive necrosis leads to fulminant failure or requires transplant.  
   Monitorable: Indirectly via LFTs, imaging (Doppler/CT), and emerging blood biomarkers (keratin-18, micro-RNA-122).  

5) Gastritis / gastric discomfort  
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
   Nature: Often NON-ADVERSE or minimally adverse; no bleeding or ulceration.  
   Reversibility: Yes – within days, aided by food co-administration or PPIs.  
   Monitorable: Clinical symptoms, optional endoscopy if severe.  

5.  UNCERTAINTIES & DATA GAPS  

•  Individual susceptibility is influenced by genetic polymorphisms (e.g., UGT1A6, CYP2E1*5B), nutritional status, alcohol intake and concomitant drugs (isoniazid, rifampicin).  
•  Limited controlled 28-day datasets at daily doses >4 g; extrapolation beyond that range increases uncertainty.  
•  Non-hepatic digestive organs (pancreas, intestinal mucosa) show little evidence of direct toxicity, but the database is relatively small.  

6.  OVERALL CONCLUSION (WEIGHT OF EVIDENCE)  

Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity toward the digestive system:  
•  GI mucosa – LOW  
•  Liver – HIGH (principal risk driver)  

At the maximum tolerated human dose of 4 g/day for 28 days, paracetamol is best characterised by:  
•  A high probability of asymptomatic, reversible hepatocellular enzyme elevations (ALT/AST).  
•  A low but clinically significant risk of progressing to clinically apparent hepatotoxicity with centrilobular necrosis, particularly in susceptible individuals or in the presence of co-factors that increase NAPQI formation or deplete glutathione.  
•  Mild, transient upper-GI symptoms that are generally adaptive and reversible.  

Clinical monitoring (symptom enquiry plus weekly liver-function tests) is adequate to detect early adverse effects, and prompt discontinuation together with NAC therapy can prevent irreversible injury.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Chemical                                    : 1,1-Dimethylbiguanide hydrochloride  
Synonyms                                     : Metformin, Metformin HCl, Glucophage®  
CAS-No.                                      : 657-24-9  

Scenario                                    : Once-daily administration of the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) to an adult human for 28 consecutive days by the oral route.  
“MTD” in clinical practice ≈ 2.5–3 g/day (≈ 35–40 mg kg-1 day-1 for a 70-kg adult). This is the highest dose that is approved and generally tolerated in Phase-III trials and post-marketing experience (FDA, 2020; EMA EPAR, 2018).  

FOCUS                                       : Digestive system (functional changes, clinical pathology, histopathology).  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Experimental data  
• >15 placebo-controlled trials, open-label extensions and post-marketing surveillance covering >10 000 patient-years (Bailey & Turner 1996; UKPDS 1998; Garber et al. 2019).  
• Animal 28-day MTD studies in rat and dog (NIDDK, 1977; EMA, 2018).  
Predictive/Mechanistic data  
• In situ intestinal perfusion, organoid cultures and Caco-2 studies (Stepensky et al. 2002; Han et al. 2021).  
• Adverse Outcome Pathway fragments linking mitochondrial complex-I inhibition → ↓ATP in enterocytes → altered ion/fluid secretion (OECD AOP 149 draft).  

No evidence of structural (histopathological) injury to the GI tract in humans at therapeutic or MTD doses, but a high incidence of functional disturbances is consistently reported.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. OBSERVED OR PREDICTED EFFECTS ON THE DIGESTIVE SYSTEM
--------------------------------------------------------------------

A. Functional (clinical) effects  
1. Diarrhoea (up to 53 % at initiation; stabilises to 5–10 % after 4 wk)  
2. Nausea / vomiting (20–30 % initially; <5 % after 4 wk)  
3. Abdominal discomfort, cramping, flatulence, dyspepsia (<10 %)  
4. Metallic taste / anorexia (≤3 %)  

B. Clinical pathology  
• Mild, transient increase in faecal lactate and bile acids (Stepensky 2002; Napolitano 2014).  
• No consistent changes in routine serum biochemistry (AST, ALT, amylase, lipase).  
• Very rare lactic acidosis (≈3 cases /100 000 patient-years; not a direct digestive effect).  

C. Histopathology (human)  
• Endoscopic biopsies from patients on chronic high-dose therapy show normal mucosal architecture (Wang et al. 2018).  
• Case reports of nonspecific superficial gastritis or colitis are isolated and confounded.  
Animal data at ≥10× human exposure also failed to reveal mucosal necrosis or inflammatory change (EMA review, 2018).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. MECHANISTIC EXPLANATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1 – High luminal concentration  
• Oral bioavailability ≈ 55 %; the unabsorbed fraction (≈ 45 %) remains in the small/large intestine at millimolar levels.

Step 2 – Cellular target  
• Reversible inhibition of mitochondrial Complex-I in enterocytes → ↓ATP, ↑AMP → activation of AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK).

Step 3 – Down-stream events  
a) Altered epithelial transport:  
   – ↓Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase activity, ↑paracellular permeability → net fluid secretion → osmotic diarrhoea.  
b) Enteroendocrine signalling:  
   – ↑GLP-1 and ↑5-HT release → nausea, early satiety, visceral hypersensitivity.  
c) Microbiome/bile-acid modulation:  
   – ↑Anaerobic carbohydrate fermentation → gas, cramps;  
   – ↓Ileal bile-acid reabsorption (via ASBT inhibition) → choleretic diarrhoea.

Step 4 – Resolution / Adaptation  
Up-regulation of transporters (OCT1, PMAT) and partial mitochondrial adaptation restore energy balance in 1–3 wk in most subjects, explaining the decline of GI symptoms over time.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. ADVERSE vs ADAPTIVE, REVERSIBILITY & CLINICAL MONITORING
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Effect                                    | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
-------------------------------------------|------------------|-------------|------------------------
Diarrhoea, nausea, abdominal pain          | Adverse (symptomatic discomfort, may affect compliance) | Yes – typically within days after dose reduction/cessation | Patient-reported outcomes; stool frequency/consistency
Metallic taste / anorexia                  | Mostly adaptive, minor | Yes | Weight, food intake
Transient ↑faecal lactate & bile acids     | Adaptive biomarker | Yes | Not routinely needed
Lactic acidosis (extremely rare, systemic) | Adverse, life-threatening | Potentially with intensive care | Blood lactate, pH, bicarbonate (standard of care in risk patients)
Histological GI changes                    | None expected at MTD | N/A | Endoscopy only if symptomatic; not standard

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. SYSTEMIC AVAILABILITY & TOXICODYNAMIC ACTIVITY RATINGS
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Systemic availability: Low-to-Medium  
• Oral F ≈ 55 %, negligible protein binding, rapid renal clearance (t½ 4–6 h).

Toxicodynamic activity in GI tract: Medium-to-High  
• High local luminal concentrations, direct mitochondrial interaction, microbiome effects.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. UNCERTAINTIES / DATA GAPS
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Limited quantitative human biopsy data after short-term high-dose exposure.  
• Genetic variability in transporters (OCT1 loss-of-function) may increase luminal levels and GI intolerance but remains insufficiently characterised.  
• Animal models inadequately mimic human microbiome-driven effects.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. CONCLUSION
--------------------------------------------------------------------
At the human MTD administered orally for 28 days, metformin is expected to cause common, functionally adverse but non-serious gastrointestinal effects (diarrhoea, nausea, abdominal discomfort). These disturbances are mechanistically linked to local mitochondrial complex-I inhibition, altered epithelial transport and microbiome/bile-acid interactions. They are reversible, clinically monitorable, and not associated with histopathological injury to the digestive tract. The weight of evidence does not support structural GI toxicity at the MTD, but individual intolerance may necessitate dose reduction or cessation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Digestive system  
Test article: Atorvastatin calcium (synonyms: Lipitor®, CI-981; CAS 134523-00-5)  
Scenario: Maximum tolerated dose (MTD)1 given once-daily p.o. to an adult human for 28 consecutive days.

1  In clinical development the “pharmacological” MTD in healthy adults was 80 mg/day (≈ 1.1 mg kg-1 day-1 for a 70-kg person). Pre-marketing repeat-dose safety studies in humans and in animals (rat, dog, monkey) escalated to higher multiples; findings reported below derive from those data sets and the current post-marketing database.

Summary conclusion  
a. The gastrointestinal (GI) tract itself shows only mild, largely adaptive disturbances (dyspepsia, diarrhoea, nausea).  
b. The liver (a digestive system organ) is the principal target; effects range from adaptive enzyme induction to occasionally adverse, idiosyncratic hepatocellular injury.  
c. All effects observed at, or even above, the clinical MTD are reversible after cessation and can be readily monitored by routine clinical chemistry.

SYSTEMIC AVAILABILITY & TOXICODYNAMIC POTENTIAL  
• Oral absorption ≈ 30 %; extensive first-pass extraction → hepatic exposure high, systemic (post-hepatic) exposure moderate (bioavailability 12 %).  
• Protein binding > 98 %.  
Classification:  
– Systemic availability for liver: HIGH (by design, first-pass targeting).  
– Systemic availability for extra-hepatic tissues: LOW–MEDIUM.  
– Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity on the digestive system: MEDIUM (largely mechanism-based and reversible).

DETAILED EFFECTS, MECHANISMS AND CLINICAL CHARACTERISTICS

1. Upper & lower GI tract  
Observed / reported effects at MTD  
• Dyspepsia, epigastric discomfort, abdominal pain, flatulence, diarrhoea, constipation, nausea, vomiting. Incidence 2–8 % in controlled trials; severity usually Grade 1–2.  
• In dogs given 120 mg kg-1 day-1 for 3 months, vomiting and soft faeces were the only GI clinical signs; no histopathological lesions of stomach or intestines (Food and Drug Administration [FDA] review, 1996).  
Mechanistic interpretation  
– Local irritant/osmotic effect of the calcium salt.  
– Inhibition of cholesterol synthesis in enterocytes → reduced chylomicron secretion may transiently alter intestinal motility.  
Adverse vs. adaptive: Generally non-adverse, self-limiting functional changes.  
Reversibility: Yes, within 1–3 days after discontinuation.  
Monitorability: Symptom reporting; no specific lab marker required.

2. Hepatocellular changes  
2.1. Serum transaminase elevations  
Findings  
• Persistent (> 2× upper limit of normal, ULN) ALT and/or AST increases in ≈ 0.7 % of subjects at 80 mg (Pfizer global safety database; FDA label).  
• In a 26-week healthy volunteer study, mean ALT rose < 15 % vs. baseline; individual maxima 3–5× ULN.  
Histopathology (preclinical)  
• Rat: at 100 mg kg-1 day-1 (≈ 25× human AUC) – centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy and vacuolation without necrosis (adaptive).  
• Dog: 120 mg kg-1 day-1 – minimal hepatocyte single-cell necrosis, reversible after 4-week recovery.  
Mechanism  
Primary, on-target: HMG-CoA reductase inhibition ↓ hepatic cholesterol → ↑ SREBP-mediated enzyme/genome responses → hepatocellular hypertrophy; secondary mitochondrial stress and reactive metabolite formation via CYP3A4 can, in rare individuals, trigger idiosyncratic necrosis.  
Adverse vs. adaptive  
– Mild, transient ALT/AST ≤ 3× ULN: adaptive.  
– Persistent > 3× ULN or with bilirubin elevation (“Hy’s law”): adverse.  
Reversibility: Yes, median 2–6 weeks post-drug.  
Monitorability: Serum ALT, AST, total bilirubin.

2.2. Cholestatic or mixed-pattern liver injury (rare, ≤ 1/10 000)  
Mechanism  
Likely immune-mediated or due to bile acid transporter interference (BSEP inhibition demonstrated in vitro; IC50 ≈ 35 µM – achievable only under MTD + drug-interaction conditions).  
Adverse, reversible in most reported cases once drug stopped; monitorable by ALP, γ-GT, conjugated bilirubin.

3. Pancreas  
Clinical reports of acute pancreatitis exist (< 0.1 %) but causal link uncertain (confounding gall-stone disease, alcohol). No pancreatic lesions in 2-year rat or dog studies up to 400 mg kg-1 day-1. Weight-of-evidence suggests no direct toxic effect at MTD; if it occurs it is considered idiosyncratic and adverse.

4. Coagulation of bile / gall-bladder effects  
Statins lower biliary cholesterol saturation; may actually protect against cholesterol gallstones. No adverse gall-bladder pathology noted in repeat-dose studies.

WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE STATEMENT  
• Human clinical trials (n > 14 000) and > 25 years of pharmacovigilance are the most relevant data.  
• Preclinical species show similar or greater hepatic sensitivity, supporting human relevance.  
• In silico QSAR alerts for reactive quinone-type metabolites (via para-hydroxy atorvastatin) align with rare idiosyncratic liver injury cases but not with routine transaminase rises.

UNCERTAINTIES & DATA GAPS  
• Precise dose–response for individual susceptibility to idiosyncratic hepatotoxicity remains undefined.  
• The highest safe exposure in individuals with pre-existing NAFLD or CYP3A4 inhibitors may be lower than the general MTD.

OVERALL CLASSIFICATION OF DIGESTIVE-SYSTEM TOXICITY AT THE 28-DAY MTD

1. Functional GI upset – LOW severity, non-adverse, reversible.  
2. Hepatocellular enzyme elevation – MEDIUM, usually adaptive; can be adverse in a small sub-population.  
3. Hepatic necrosis / cholestatic injury – HIGH severity but VERY LOW frequency; clearly adverse, reversible in most cases.  
4. Other digestive organs – No consistent toxicity.

CLINICAL MONITORING RECOMMENDATIONS  
• Baseline and periodic (e.g., week 2 and week 4) ALT, AST, total and direct bilirubin.  
• Symptom query for abdominal pain, nausea, pruritus, dark urine (signals for serious liver injury).  
• Discontinue if ALT/AST > 3× ULN with symptoms or bilirubin > 2× ULN.

REFERENCES (representative)  
– FDA Center for Drug Evaluation and Research. Clinical Pharmacology and Biopharmaceutics Review of Atorvastatin (NDA 20-702), 1996.  
– European Medicines Agency EPAR – Lipitor, 2004 update.  
– Björnsson E. Statins and liver injury: molecular mechanisms and clinical lessons. Hepatology 2017;66:1511-26.  
– Zimmerman HJ. Hepatotoxicity, 2nd ed. Lippincott Williams & Wilkins, 1999.  
– PubChem CID 60823; toxicology summaries.  

The assessment indicates that, at the human MTD for 28 days, atorvastatin produces mainly mild, reversible digestive effects, with the liver being the only organ where toxicity can occasionally reach an adverse level.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Prednisone (synonyms: Deltasone®, Meticorten®, CAS 53-03-2)  
Scenario: Maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) administered orally, once daily, for 28 consecutive days to an adult human; focus on the digestive system.

1. Systemic availability (toxicokinetics)  
• Oral bioavailability: 70–90 % (prodrug rapidly converted in the liver to the active metabolite prednisolone).  
• cmax reached in 1–2 h; t½ prednisolone ≈ 3 h, but genomic effects last ≥24 h.  
• High plasma exposure over 28 days → HIGH systemic availability classification.

2. Weight-of-evidence for digestive-system findings  

2.1 Functional (clinical) effects that are well documented in humans  
a) Dyspepsia, epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting (incidence 5–15 %; DailyMed 2023, UpToDate 2024).  
b) Peptic-ulcer disease (PUD) and gastrointestinal (GI) bleeding, especially when co-administered with NSAIDs, H. pylori, or alcohol (OR ≈ 2–4; Conn & Poynard 1994; Narum 2014 meta-analysis).  
c) Esophageal candidiasis or reflux esophagitis (immunosuppression, decreased LES tone).  
d) Acute pancreatitis (rare, <1 %; Einarson 2022 case review).  
e) Elevations in serum aminotransferases, rarely drug-induced steatohepatitis or cholestatic hepatitis (Björnsson 2013; LiverTox).

2.2 Histopathology / endoscopic findings  
• Stomach/duodenum: superficial erosions to deep ulcers with inflammatory infiltrate, fibrinoid necrosis and occasional perforation.  
• Liver: microvesicular or macrovesicular steatosis, mild portal inflammation; rare centrilobular necrosis.  
• Pancreas: acinar cell vacuolisation and focal fat necrosis in pancreatitis.

Evidence sources: human endoscopic series (Boers 1996), rat 28-day GLP study (NOAEL 5 mg/kg; erosive gastritis at ≥25 mg/kg; ECHA dossier), case biopsies (LiverTox).

3. Mechanistic explanation of digestive effects  

3.1 Gastric/duodenal mucosal injury  
• Glucocorticoid receptor–mediated inhibition of phospholipase A2 → ↓ arachidonic acid → ↓ protective prostaglandins E2/I2.  
• Suppressed epithelial turnover and angiogenesis → impaired mucosal repair.  
• Up-regulation of gastric acid secretion (indirect via gastrin) and ↓ mucus/bicarbonate barrier.  
• Synergism with NSAID-induced COX inhibition explains high ulcer risk when combined.

3.2 GI bleeding/perforation  
• Ulcer depth can reach submucosal vessels; concurrent inhibition of platelet aggregation (↓ thromboxane) prolongs bleeding.  
• Steroid-induced thinning of collagen makes perforation more likely.

3.3 Pancreatitis  
• Proposed mechanisms: 1) hypertriglyceridaemia induced by steroids, 2) direct toxic effect on acinar cells via oxidative stress, 3) increased viscosity of pancreatic secretions.

3.4 Hepatic injury  
• Prednisolone is largely metabolised by CYP3A4; reactive oxygen species from microsomal metabolism plus altered lipid handling lead to steatosis.  
• Immune suppression may predispose to viral reactivation (HBV) causing secondary hepatitis.

3.5 Esophageal candidiasis  
• Local immune suppression and gastric reflux allow Candida overgrowth.

4. Adversity, reversibility and monitorability  

Effect | Adverse vs. adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
-------|----------------------|-------------|----------------------
Dyspepsia / mild gastritis | Generally adaptive/irritative, may precede ulcer | Yes, resolves on dose reduction or PPIs | Symptom check, urea breath test, endoscopy if persistent
Peptic ulcer & GI bleeding | Adverse | Usually reversible with therapy; risk of sequelae (perforation) | Fecal occult blood, Hb/Hct, endoscopy
Pancreatitis | Adverse | Usually reversible; may be severe | Serum amylase/lipase, abdominal imaging, clinical signs
Transient ALT/AST ↑ | Usually adaptive, can be adverse if >3× ULN or bilirubin ↑ | Reversible; rare chronic injury | Liver panel
Hepatosteatosis/hepatitis | Adverse | Often reversible, may take weeks–months | Imaging (US/MRI), liver panel
Esophageal candidiasis | Adverse but minor | Reversible with antifungals | Endoscopy, fungal culture

5. Toxicodynamic activity in the digestive system  
Given the documented ulcerogenic potential, capacity to induce pancreatitis, and hepatic effects, the toxicodynamic activity is classified as MEDIUM-to-HIGH. The severity depends strongly on cofactors (NSAIDs, alcohol, comorbidities).

6. Uncertainties / data gaps  
• No controlled 28-day MTD human trials exist; inference comes from therapeutic-dose cohorts and post-marketing surveillance.  
• Animal data show GI lesions at doses ≥5-fold human equivalence; translation to humans carries uncertainty.  
• Mechanistic details for steroid-induced pancreatitis remain speculative.

7. Risk-management / mitigation (clinical relevance)  
• Co-prescribe proton-pump inhibitor or H2 antagonist at MTD.  
• Screen/eradicate H. pylori prior to long courses.  
• Avoid or minimise NSAID/alcohol co-exposure.  
• Monitor CBC, liver enzymes, serum lipids, and GI symptoms at baseline and weekly.  
• Educate patients on warning signs (melena, hematemesis, severe abdominal pain).  

Overall conclusion  
A 28-day oral MTD course of prednisone is expected to produce HIGH systemic exposure and a MEDIUM-to-HIGH probability of adverse digestive-system effects. Most effects (dyspepsia, mild enzyme elevations) are reversible and clinically monitorable; serious outcomes such as ulcers with bleeding or pancreatitis are less common but clearly adverse, necessitating proactive gastro-protection and laboratory surveillance.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”)  
CAS No.: 1746-01-6  

Scenario to be evaluated  
• Adult human, 28-day repeated oral administration at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD).  
• Target: digestive system (gastro-intestinal tract sensu strictu plus hepatobiliary and pancreatic organs).  

1. Systemic availability and toxicodynamic potency  
• Physicochemical properties: logKow ≈ 6.8, water solubility < 0.02 mg L-1, vapor pressure 3.4 × 10-10 mm Hg.  
• ADME: Very well absorbed with dietary fats (> 50 % in rodents; limited human data but similar trend). Extensive distribution to lipid-rich tissues; enterohepatic circulation; biological half-life in humans 7–11 years. Very low metabolic clearance (slow oxidation and conjugation).  
• Toxicodynamic driver: high-affinity agonism at the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR); human Kd ≈ 0.1 nM.  
→ Systemic availability: HIGH (bio-persistent, accumulative).  
→ Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (sub-µg kg-1 day-1 can elicit responses).  

2. Choice of “MTD” for a 28-day human scenario  
Human MTD data do not exist; therefore animal data are used for a conservative extrapolation.  
• Rat 28-day NOAEL ≈ 0.01 µg kg-1 day-1; LOAEL ≈ 0.1 µg kg-1 day-1 (hepatotoxicity, wasting).  
• Doses ≥ 1 µg kg-1 day-1 approach lethal range.  
For the purpose of this qualitative description, a daily oral dose in the low µg kg-1 range is considered an “MTD” that produces clear systemic effects without frank lethality.

3. Observed / predicted digestive-system effects after 28 days  

3.1 Liver (principal target)  
Functional/clinical pathology  
• Serum ALT, AST, ALP, γ-GT, and total bilirubin ↑ (2–10-fold in animals).  
• Serum cholesterol and triglyceride ↓; hepatic triglyceride content ↑ (fatty liver).  
• Plasma albumin ↓; prothrombin time ↑.  

Histopathology  
• Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (due to CYP1A1/1A2 induction) – partly adaptive.  
• Macro- and microvesicular steatosis.  
• Bile duct hyperplasia, periportal cholestasis; occasionally pigment accumulation (greenish-brown bile).  
• Mild-to-moderate inflammatory cell infiltrates; progression to apoptotic/necrotic foci at high doses.  
• Early periportal fibrosis reported in 13-week studies at ≥ 0.5 µg kg-1 day-1 in rats.

Mechanism  
AhR activation → altered transcription of genes controlling xenobiotic metabolism (CYP1A), lipid homeostasis (PPARα, SREBP-1c), bile-acid transporters (BSEP, MRP2) → oxidative stress (ROS via CYP induction) + disrupted lipid and bile-acid trafficking → steatosis and cholestasis. Inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β) further drive cell injury.

Adversity & reversibility  
• Hepatocellular hypertrophy: usually adaptive and reversible (weeks–months).  
• Steatosis, cholestasis, necrosis, fibrosis: adverse. Steatosis is largely reversible on cessation; early fibrosis may regress partially; necrosis/bridging fibrosis may leave residual scarring.  

Clinical monitoring  
Liver enzymes, bilirubin, bile acids, ultrasound elastography, MRI-PDFF (fat quantification), coagulation panel.

3.2 Gall-bladder and bile ducts  
Findings  
• Bile duct proliferation (microscopic).  
• Thickened gall-bladder wall, decreased gall-bladder filling in rats/dogs.

Mechanism  
Cholestatic pressure and AhR-induced epithelial proliferation.

Adversity & reversibility  
Adverse; regression likely after dosing stops if fibrosis has not developed.

3.3 Pancreas  
Findings  
• Decreased pancreatic weight (10–30 % in 4-week rat studies).  
• Acinar atrophy, reduced zymogen granules; serum amylase & lipase ↓.  
• Secondary hypoinsulinemia and fasting hypoglycaemia occasionally reported (rat).

Mechanism  
AhR-mediated repression of digestive enzyme genes; energy-balance disturbance due to wasting syndrome.

Adversity & reversibility  
Adverse; acinar atrophy reversible within weeks in rodents.

Clinical monitoring  
Faecal elastase, serum amylase/lipase, abdominal ultrasound.

3.4 Gastro-intestinal tract (mucosa, motility, microbiome)  
Findings  
• Villus shortening and crypt-cell proliferation reduction in small intestine at ≥ 0.3 µg kg-1 day-1 (mouse/rat, 2–4 wks).  
• Decreased digestive enzyme activity (lactase, maltase); malabsorption-associated diarrhoea.  
• Reduced gastric emptying and intestinal transit time (prokinetic gene repression).  
• Microbiome shift toward Proteobacteria with increased LPS levels (mouse).

Mechanism  
Systemic AhR activation in intestinal stem cells → suppression of Wnt/β-catenin signalling → reduced epithelial renewal. Secondary effects from altered bile-acid pool and cytokine milieu contribute.

Adversity & reversibility  
Generally adverse if malabsorptive diarrhoea, weight loss (> 10 %) occur. Reversible within 1–2 weeks once exposure ends, provided cachexia has not progressed.

Clinical monitoring  
Body weight, stool frequency/fat, serum albumin, endoscopy if indicated.

3.5 Porphyrinogenic effects (secondary abdominal symptoms)  
Accumulation of uroporphyrins in liver → episodic abdominal pain & dark urine (observed in workers, supported by rodent data). Mechanism: Uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase inhibition downstream of AhR-mediated CYP1A2 induction generating reactive oxygen species.

4. Summary matrix  

Effect | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?  
Hepatocellular hypertrophy | Adaptive | Yes | Ultrasound, ALP, CYP1A biomarker (urinary 1-OH-pyrene)  
Steatosis | Adverse | Largely yes | ALT/AST, MRI-PDFF  
Cholestasis / bile-duct proliferation | Adverse | Partial | Bilirubin, ALP, γ-GT, ultrasound  
Necrosis / early fibrosis | Adverse | Partially | Liver enzymes, ELF score, FibroScan  
Pancreatic acinar atrophy | Adverse | Yes | Faecal elastase, amylase/lipase  
GI villus atrophy / diarrhoea | Adverse | Yes | Weight, stool fat, endoscopy  
Porphyria-related abdominal pain | Adverse | Yes (months) | Plasma/urine porphyrins  

5. Weight-of-evidence and uncertainties  
• Robust animal database (mice, rats, guinea pigs, dogs, non-human primates) consistently shows liver as primary digestive target.  
• Epidemiological evidence (Yusho/Yucheng and occupational cohorts) supports hepatobiliary enzyme changes and porphyria but is confounded by mixed exposures.  
• Human inter-individual variability (CYP1A2 polymorphisms, body-fat content) introduces uncertainty in dose–response extrapolation.  
• The assumed MTD (~µg kg-1 day-1) may underestimate chronic body burden achievable via bioaccumulation beyond 28 days.  
• Data on direct gastric or colonic histopathology in humans are lacking; predictions rely on rodent studies and mechanistic plausibility.

6. Overall conclusion  
At an MTD-level 28-day repeated oral exposure, TCDD is predicted to cause pronounced hepatobiliary and secondary pancreatic/GI toxicity in humans. Most initial adaptive changes (enzyme induction, hepatocellular hypertrophy) can regress, but steatosis, cholestasis, and early fibrotic changes are clearly adverse. All key effects are mechanistically linked to sustained high-affinity AhR activation coupled with the compound’s exceptional systemic persistence. Clinical surveillance would focus on liver biochemistry, imaging for steatosis/fibrosis, pancreatic function tests, and monitoring of GI symptoms and nutritional status.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Benzene (synonyms: cyclohexatriene, benzol)  
CAS No.: 71-43-2  
Scenario: Daily oral administration to an adult human at the maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD) for 28 consecutive days (sub-acute study focus)  
Target: Digestive system (oral cavity → stomach → intestine; accessory organs: liver, biliary tree, pancreas).  

1. Systemic availability (ADME)

• Absorption: High. Human volunteers given 0.5 mg/kg benzene in gelatin capsules absorbed >90 % of the dose within 1 h (Snyder 1989; ATSDR 2022). Log Kow ≈ 2.1 and complete miscibility with lipids facilitate rapid passive diffusion across gastric and intestinal mucosa.  
• Distribution: Quickly enters portal blood; peak liver concentrations within minutes in rodents (Sabourin 1987).  
• Metabolism: Predominantly in liver by CYP2E1 → benzene-oxide → phenol, catechol, hydroquinone, 1,2,4-benzenetriol, muconaldehyde. Phase-II conjugation (glucuronides, sulfates, mercapturates).  
• Excretion: Metabolites cleared in urine (≥70 %), CO₂ in expired air (≈20 %), minor fecal loss.  
→ Systemic availability rating: High (efficient GI absorption and slow first-pass elimination).

2. Weight-of-evidence relevant to the digestive tract

Human data  
• Controlled oral data are limited to single low doses; no repeated MTD studies for ethical reasons. Only transient nausea or gastritis reported at accidental ingestions (Rothman 1996).  
• Occupational/epidemiological studies focus on inhalation; digestive findings are not prominent.

Animal repeat-dose oral studies  
1. NTP 15-week gavage study (TR-289, mice & rats; corn-oil vehicle)  
   – Doses up to 600 mg/kg bw/day.  
   – Main findings: bone-marrow toxicity; no gross stomach/intestinal lesions; mild hepatocellular hypertrophy at ≥150 mg/kg.  
2. Sub-chronic drinking-water studies (Wolf 1956; ECHA dossier)  
   – Rats 0, 25, 100 mg/kg/day for 90 d: ↑ relative liver weight (dose-related), minimal centrilobular hypertrophy; stomach and intestinal histology unremarkable.  
3. Repeated gavage studies in B6C3F1 mice (Ward 1985)  
   – 200–500 mg/kg/day/90 d: fatty change and single-cell necrosis in liver of a minority of animals; increased ALT/AST; no forestomach hyperplasia.  
Overall conclusion: The digestive tract is not a primary benzene target. When the MTD is approached, the only consistent digestive-system findings are mild, mostly adaptive, hepatic changes.

3. Expected digestive-system effects in a 28-day human MTD scenario

3.1 Oral cavity, esophagus, stomach, intestine  
a. Local irritation (gastritis, nausea, possible emesis)  
   • Mechanism: direct lipid-solvent effect on mucosal phospholipids → increased membrane permeability → sensory nerve stimulation.  
   • Evidence: Reports of abdominal pain after acute oral poisoning (>10 mL). Rodent gavage often produces transient gastric irritation at ≥200 mg/kg.  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: Adverse (functional disturbance).  
   • Reversibility: Rapid (24–48 h) once exposure ceases; mucosa regenerates quickly.  
   • Clinical monitoring: Patient-reported GI symptoms, endoscopy only if clinically indicated.

b. No consistent histopathology (no erosions, no hyperplasia) expected at MTD that does not yet compromise systemic health.

3.2 Liver (accessory digestive organ)  
a. Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy  
   • Mechanism: CYP2E1 induction by benzene/phenol → adaptive proliferation of smooth endoplasmic reticulum.  
   • Evidence: ↑ liver weight (+10–20 %) and microscopic hypertrophy in rodents ≥150 mg/kg (NTP 1986; Kalf 1987; ECHA 2023).  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: Primarily adaptive; not associated with necrosis or fibrosis at 28 d.  
   • Reversibility: High; regresses within 7–14 d after cessation as CYP levels return to baseline.  
   • Monitorable: Serum ALP, GGT (often normal), ultrasound for liver size.

b. Mild hepatocellular single-cell necrosis / lipid accumulation (only at the upper edge of the MTD)  
   • Mechanism: Reactive metabolites (benzene-oxide, hydroquinone) generate ROS, deplete GSH → oxidative stress, lipid peroxidation.  
   • Evidence: Seen in 90-d mouse study at ≥400 mg/kg; sporadic in shorter studies.  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: Adverse (cell death).  
   • Reversibility: Usually reversible via hepatic regeneration if exposure stops.  
   • Monitorable: Small, transient ↑ ALT/AST (up to 2–3-fold).

3.3 Biliary tract & pancreas  
• No treatment-related changes demonstrated in repeated-dose studies up to 600 mg/kg; no mechanistic expectation of direct toxicity.  
• Prediction: Low likelihood of clinically meaningful cholestasis or pancreatitis in a 28-day MTD scenario.

4. Toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability ratings (digestive system)  
• Systemic availability: High (efficient absorption, distribution to liver).  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward digestive tract: Low–Medium  
  – Low for luminal GI tract (mainly irritation at very high doses).  
  – Medium for liver (adaptive hypertrophy; mild oxidative stress at MTD).

5. Adverse Outcome Pathway (AOP) coherence  
Molecular initiating event: CYP2E1-mediated oxidation of benzene → benzene-oxide.  
Key events (digestive system context):  
  1. Enzyme induction (adaptive).  
  2. ROS generation / GSH depletion (threshold-dependent).  
  3. Cellular stress response → Nrf2 activation (adaptive).  
  4. If overwhelmed: single-cell necrosis (adverse).  
Outcome: Transient mild hepatotoxicity; minimal impact on other digestive tissues.

6. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• No human 28-day oral MTD data; extrapolated from rodent data and accidental ingestion.  
• Tablet or capsule formulation could reduce local irritation versus gavage bolus in animals.  
• Interspecies scaling of CYP2E1 induction and glutathione capacity introduces uncertainty.  
• Potential microbiome interactions have not been studied.

7. Overall conclusion (digestive system only)  
At an orally administered 28-day MTD, benzene is expected to:  
• Produce high systemic availability.  
• Cause, at most, mild, reversible mucosal irritation (gastritis, nausea).  
• Induce adaptive centrilobular hepatic hypertrophy with possible small increases in serum liver enzymes; occasional single-cell necrosis cannot be excluded at the high end of the MTD.  
• Effects are monitorable (clinical signs, serum chemistry, imaging) and are expected to be reversible upon cessation.  
• No serious, progressive, or fibrotic digestive-system pathology is anticipated; major toxicity will continue to reside in the hematopoietic system rather than the GI tract.

Key references  
• NTP Technical Report 289. Benzene (CAS No. 71-43-2) in F344/N rats and B6C3F1 mice (15-week gavage). 1986.  
• ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Benzene. 2022.  
• ECHA Registration Dossier for Benzene. 2023.  
• Sabourin PJ et al. “Dynamics of benzene metabolism in mice.” Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1987.  
• Ward CO et al. “Subchronic oral study of benzene in mice and rats.” Fund Appl Toxicol 1985.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation: arsenite (commonly administered as sodium arsenite, NaAsO2, CAS-No. 15502-74-6)

Exposure scenario under consideration
• Adult human, once-daily oral administration of a “maximum tolerated dose” (MTD) for 28 consecutive days  
• Focus of the assessment: digestive (gastro-intestinal, hepatobiliary and pancreato-intestinal) system

Key reference sources
• ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic (update 2022)  
• ECHA Registration Dossier: sodium arsenite (2023 update)  
• NTP. Toxicology and Carcinogenesis Studies of Sodium Arsenite (2014)  
• IARC Monograph 100C – Arsenic and Inorganic Arsenic Compounds (2012)  
• OECD TG 407 repeat-dose 28-day studies in rats and mice (summarised in ECHA dossier)

1. Systemic availability (ADME) – weight of evidence
• Oral absorption of inorganic arsenite in mammals is rapid and efficient (70–95 %, depending on matrix; Hughes 2002).  
• Peak blood concentrations reached in 0.5–2 h; volume of distribution ≈ 0.6 L kg-1 suggests ready access to most soft tissues, including the gastro-intestinal mucosa and liver.  
• Metabolism: sequential oxidative methylation in liver (AsIII → MMA → DMA) catalysed by arsenic-methyl-transferase (AS3MT); methylated metabolites circulate back to GI tract via bile.  
• Elimination t½ ≈ 10–30 h (blood), but tissue retention in liver, intestinal epithelium and keratinised tissues occurs.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability – HIGH.

2. Digestive-system hazard profile at MTD (28-day, repeated)
The table below integrates human clinical case-series, controlled animal studies at/near MTD, and in-silico mechanistic data. “MTD” corresponds in rodents to 3–10 mg As/kg bw day, producing ≤10 % body-weight loss without excess mortality (NTP 2014). Human MTD extrapolated allometrically is ~0.3–0.5 mg As/kg bw day.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Observed / predicted finding           Evidence (human, animal)            Mechanistic explanation            Adverse?   Reversible?   Clinically monitorable?
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Acute-onset gastroduodenitis with nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain and watery to bloody diarrhoea beginning Day 1–2 and persisting intermittently throughout dosing.   • Recurrent clinical reports in therapeutic arsenic trioxide regimens (0.15 mg As/kg bw day for 4 weeks); • 28-day rat/mouse sodium arsenite studies show epithelial necrosis, oedema, mucosal erosion at ≥5 mg/kg bw day.   • Direct cytotoxicity via AsIII interaction with protein thiols in mitochondrial PDH, disruption of ATP production → epithelial energy failure; • ROS generation → oxidative injury; • Local inflammatory cytokine induction (IL-8, TNF-α).   Adverse.   Yes – GI mucosa regenerates in 3–7 days post-cessation if no perforation.   Yes – symptoms, stool occult blood, endoscopy, faecal calprotectin.
2. Small intestinal malabsorption: steatorrhoea, weight loss; villus blunting and crypt hyperplasia histologically.   • Reported in chronic arsenic-poisoning patients (Mazumder 2005); • Rat 28-day study: decreased villus height, reduced brush-border enzymes at 5 mg/kg day.   • Same mitochondrial/ROS mechanism; additional inhibition of Na+/K+-ATPase and tight-junction proteins → barrier dysfunction.   Adverse.   Largely reversible within weeks if epithelial stem-cell compartment not destroyed.   Yes – body weight, serum albumin, D-xylose absorption test.
3. Hepatotoxicity: elevated ALT/AST, bilirubin; histology – centrilobular vacuolation, mild necrosis, cholangio-hepatitis; occasional cholestasis.   • Numerous case reports in therapeutic arsenic trioxide; • NTP rat study (28-day, 10 mg/kg bw day): ↑ ALT 3- to 5-fold, microvesicular steatosis.   • Arsenite biotransformation in liver produces ROS and As-glutathione complexes → mitochondrial permeability transition, lipid peroxidation; • Impaired β-oxidation and bile-salt export pump inhibition.   Adverse.   Often reversible within 2–6 weeks; progression to fibrosis unlikely after 28 days.   Yes – serum liver panel, ultrasound elastography.
4. Pancreatic exocrine stress: transient ↑ serum amylase/lipase; minimal acinar vacuolation.   • Observed in 90-day rat studies at ≥2.5 mg/kg day; rare human case reports.   • Oxidative stress in acinar cells; secondary to cholestatic bile flow disturbance.   Mild adverse.   Reversible.   Yes – serum lipase/amylase.
5. Altered gut microbiota / bile-acid pool (inferred).   • 14-day mouse study (Lu et al. 2020) showed ↓ Firmicutes, ↑ Proteobacteria at 2 mg/kg day.   • Arsenite acts as antimicrobial and selects for arsenic-resistance operon-bearing taxa; altered bile acids feed back on mucosal health.   Adaptive/uncertain adversity.   Reversible within 1–2 weeks.   Potential biomarker research stage (metagenomics).
6. Gastro-intestinal haemorrhage / anaemia (in severe MTD excursions).   • Case reports of occult GI bleeding with therapeutic arsenic; • Rat 28-day MTD: gastric ulceration and haemorrhage present in 10-20 % animals.   • Combination of mucosal necrosis, endothelial oxidative injury, platelet inhibition via As-induced NO​•.   Adverse.   Reversible if exposure stops; may need supportive care.   Yes – faecal occult blood, Hb/Hct.

3. Mechanistic integration (adverse outcome pathway perspective)
Molecular initiating event: direct binding of AsIII to vicinal di-thiol groups in mitochondrial and cytosolic proteins (e.g., lipoamide-containing enzymes) →  
Key events: 1) Mitochondrial dysfunction / ATP depletion; 2) Excess ROS and oxidative DNA/protein damage; 3) Activation of inflammatory signalling (NF-κB, NLRP3); 4) Loss of epithelial barrier integrity → fluid loss, ulceration; 5) Hepatocellular oxidative stress → necrosis/steatosis.  
Adverse outcomes: clinical GI irritation, malabsorption, hepatic injury.

4. Classification of toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability for the digestive system
• Toxicodynamic potency: HIGH (effective at low-mg/kg-day range; steep dose–response).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid and near-complete absorption, enterohepatic cycling).

5. Uncertainties and data gaps
• No controlled 28-day MTD study in humans; extrapolation relies on therapeutic arsenic trioxide experience and animal studies.  
• Inter-individual variability in AS3MT activity (genetic polymorphisms) may modulate severity.  
• Limited information on pancreatic and microbiome effects in humans.

6. Overall toxicological conclusion for 28-day oral MTD exposure
Arsenite produces unequivocally adverse effects in the digestive system at doses approaching the MTD. The primary manifestations—gastroduodenitis, intestinal villus injury, and hepatocellular damage—are mechanistically linked to arsenite’s high affinity for protein thiols and consequent mitochondrial/oxidative stress. Most lesions are likely to resolve upon cessation of exposure, but they are clearly adverse, clinically monitorable, and should be avoided by maintaining exposures well below the MTD.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Chemical: Methylmercury (CH₃–Hg⁺; CAS 16056-34-1; synonyms: methylmercuric ion, methylmercury hydroxide, methylmercury cation)  
Principal reference sources: ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Mercury (2022 update); WHO Environmental Health Criteria 101 (1990); US-EPA IRIS file (methylmercury); ECHA disseminated registration dossier; NTP 28-day gavage study in rats and mice (TR-593); multiple primary publications cited below.

1. Systemic availability after oral exposure  
• Oral absorption in adults is ≥90 % (McDowell et al., 1990; WHO, 1990).  
• Peak blood concentrations are reached within 1–4 h; >95 % of circulating CH₃Hg is bound to erythrocyte hemoglobin and low-molecular-weight cysteine conjugates.  
• Enterohepatic cycling → prolonged retention (t₁/₂ ≈ 45–65 days).  
Classification: Systemic availability – HIGH.

2. Weight-of-evidence for digestive-system toxicity during a 28-day MTD regimen  

A. Functional / clinical effects  
a) Gastro-intestinal irritation: nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps, metallic taste, diarrhea reported in adult poisoning cases at comparable daily intakes of 0.1–0.3 mg Hg/kg bw (Cernichiari et al., 1995; Harada, 1995). These signs appear within the first week and usually subside when exposure ceases.  
Mechanism: direct electrophilic binding of CH₃Hg⁺ to sulfhydryl groups on epithelial proteins → membrane disruption + local prostaglandin-mediated hyper-secretion.  
Adversity: ADVERSE (symptomatic toxicity).  
Reversibility: Generally REVERSIBLE within days to weeks post-exposure provided no corrosive ulceration has occurred.  
Clinical monitoring: interview for GI symptoms, stool frequency; faecal occult blood; endoscopy if persistent pain/bleeding.

b) Disturbance of intestinal motility (constipation alternating with diarrhea) – documented in Taiji Minamata cohort (Matsumoto et al., 1965).  
Mechanism: autonomic neuropathy of the myenteric plexus secondary to systemic neurotoxicity; in animal studies reduced acetylcholinesterase activity in intestinal wall (Kostial et al., 1999).  
Adversity: ADVERSE.  
Reversibility: PARTIALLY REVERSIBLE; can persist for months in severe cases.  
Monitoring: transit-time tests; abdominal radiography; autonomic function tests.

B. Clinical pathology  
a) Serum liver enzymes – ALT, AST, ALP, γ-GT elevations (1.5- to 4-fold) seen in 28-day rat studies at 1–3 mg Hg/kg bw/d (NTP, 2011) and in human case reports (Kajiwara et al., 1996).  
b) Moderate hyperbilirubinaemia & prolonged prothrombin time at higher doses.  
Adversity: ADVERSE once outside reference range.  
Reversibility: Generally REVERSIBLE within 4–8 weeks after cessation, assuming no massive necrosis.  
Monitoring: routine liver panel, coagulation profile.

C. Histopathology (animal 28-day studies; limited human biopsy data)  
1) Stomach/upper intestine  
• Multifocal erosions, inflammatory cell infiltrates, oedema in forestomach and glandular stomach at ≥1 mg Hg/kg/d in rats (NTP, 2011; Charbonneau et al., 1976).  
• Degeneration of parietal cells, increased mucus cell hypertrophy – interpreted as direct corrosive/irritant action and adaptive mucosal defense.  
Adversity:  
– Erosions/ulcers: ADVERSE.  
– Mucus cell hypertrophy: ADAPTIVE protective response.  
Reversibility: Erosions – REVERSIBLE with mucosal healing (3–7 days), hypertrophy – REVERSIBLE.  
Monitoring: endoscopy, faecal occult blood.

2) Liver  
• Centrilobular hepatocyte swelling, micro- & macro-vesicular steatosis, scattered single-cell necrosis; Kupffer-cell hyperplasia; occasional bile duct proliferation (rats, mice, cats).  
Mechanistic explanation:  
   – Oxidative stress (•OH, O₂•⁻) due to mitochondrial disruption.  
   – Depletion of glutathione and binding of CH₃Hg to thiol enzymes.  
   – Intracellular Ca²⁺ dys-homeostasis → cell death pathways.  
Adverse classification: ADVERSE.  
Reversibility: Dose-dependent; typically REVERSIBLE at MTD levels once exposure ends; fibrosis is not a typical outcome in 28-day period.  
Monitoring: ALT/AST, ultrasound elastography if needed.

3) Pancreas  
• Minimal acinar vacuolation and decreased digestive enzyme content reported in rats ≥2 mg Hg/kg/d for 4 weeks (Tanaka et al., 1998). Mechanism probably secondary to oxidative stress.  
• Functional consequence in humans not well documented – considered a potential but uncertain hazard.  
Adversity: UNCERTAIN (data gap).

4) Colon/large intestine  
• No consistent microscopic lesions beyond neural-mediated motility changes. Therefore no specific adverse histopathology expected at MTD in adults.

3. Categorisation of overall toxicodynamic activity for digestive system  
Toxicodynamic activity – MEDIUM (primary target is nervous system, but direct GI irritation and secondary hepatotoxicity are significant at the MTD).

4. Summary table of key digestive-system effects during 28-day oral MTD exposure to methylmercury  

Effect | Evidence | Mechanism | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?  
GI irritation (nausea, pain, diarrhea) | Human poisoning cases; rat gavage studies | Direct epithelial sulfhydryl binding, PG release | Adverse | Yes (days–weeks) | Symptom enquiry, endoscopy  
Motility changes | Taiji cohort; animal neurohistology | Autonomic/myenteric neuropathy | Adverse | Partially yes | Transit tests  
Gastric erosions/ulcers | 28-day rat studies | Local corrosive injury | Adverse | Yes | Endoscopy, FOBT  
Mucus cell hypertrophy | Rats | Adaptive protection | Adaptive | Yes | Histology only  
Elevated liver enzymes | Human cases; 28-day rodents | Oxidative stress, Hg accumulation | Adverse | Yes | Serum ALT/AST, bilirubin  
Hepatocellular degeneration | Animal histology | As above | Adverse | Yes, unless severe | Imaging, biopsy if indicated  
Pancreatic vacuolation | Limited rat data | Oxidative stress | Uncertain | Presumed yes | Serum lipase/amylase (exploratory)  

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Human data at a clearly defined “maximum tolerated dose” are unethical; most information comes from accidental poisonings with uncertain intakes or from occupational cohorts with lower chronic doses.  
• Few controlled 28-day repeat-dose animal studies specifically explored digestive-tract histopathology; most focused on neurotoxicity.  
• Pancreatic findings are based on a single laboratory; reproducibility is unknown.  
• Inter-individual variability (genetics, selenium status, gut microbiome) may modulate both absorption and hepatic demethylation.  

6. Overall conclusion  
At an MTD administered orally for 28 days, methylmercury will be almost completely absorbed and reach high systemic levels. Although the nervous system remains the critical target, the digestive system experiences secondary but clinically important toxicity:  
• Direct mucosal irritation leading to erosions/ulcers and GI discomfort.  
• Hepatocellular injury manifested by reversible enzyme elevations and mild histopathological changes.  
• Autonomic neuropathy may alter intestinal motility.  
Most of these effects are adverse but typically reversible and monitorable in clinical settings if exposure is discontinued promptly.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Vinyl chloride (VC); synonyms: chloroethene, monochloroethylene  
CAS No.: 75-01-4  
Route / regimen considered: once-daily oral administration at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) for 28 consecutive days in an adult human.

1. Systemic availability (toxicokinetics – ADME)  
• Absorption: VC is a small (M = 62.5 g mol-1), volatile, lipophilic gas (logKow ≈ 1.3; Henry constant ≈ 1.1 × 10-2 atm m³ mol-1). In rats and mice, 35-70 % of a gavage dose is absorbed through the gastrointestinal (GI) mucosa before it is lost by volatilisation (Hathaway 2001; Reitz et al. 1996). First-pass hepatic extraction is high (up to 90 %; ATSDR 2006).  
• Distribution: Following portal vein uptake, VC concentrates in liver, lesser amounts in GI wall, fat, and rapidly perfused organs.  
• Metabolism: Almost entirely oxidised by CYP2E1 to two reactive intermediates—chloroethylene oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA). Both form DNA- and protein-adducts; CEO is in equilibrium with CAA via enzymatic and non-enzymatic rearrangement.  
• Excretion: Metabolites are eliminated primarily in urine as 2-chloro-2-hydroxyacetic acid and thiodiacetic acid; minor amounts exhaled as CO₂ or unchanged VC.

Assessment: systemic availability to the digestive system—especially the liver—is HIGH.

2. Weight-of-evidence for digestive-system toxicity after repeated oral dosing  
Human data: No controlled studies. Occupational poisoning cases (inhalation) demonstrate liver injury (steatosis, cholangitis, fibrosis, angiosarcoma). No definitive human oral data exist.  
Animal data (representative studies):  
• Til et al., 1991 – Rats, gavage, 0, 0.05, 5, 50 mg kg-¹ day-¹, 12 mo. LOAEL 5 mg kg-¹ for increased liver enzymes; hepatocellular glycogen depletion; perivenous cell proliferation.  
• Feron et al., 1983 – Rats, drinking water, 1.8, 9.4, 47 mg kg-¹ day-¹, 3 mo. Dose-related bile-duct hyperplasia and periportal fibrosis at ≥9.4 mg kg-¹.  
• Maltoni et al., 1981; Drew et al., 1984 – Mice and rats; gavage ≤100 mg kg-¹ day-¹; 52 wk. Increased incidence of hepatic hemangiosarcoma and hepatocellular carcinoma.  
No consistent lesions in stomach or intestine were reported except occasional mild mucosal irritation at very high bolus doses (>100 mg kg-¹), generally attributed to volatility-induced focal frost-like injury rather than systemic toxicity (ECHA, RAR 2003).

3. Likely digestive-system effects in an adult human given the MTD for 28 days  
Note: The true human oral MTD is not established; by convention, it is the highest dose producing no life-threatening toxicity but some minimal reversible change. Extrapolating from rat NOAEL (0.05 mg kg-¹ day-¹) and applying default interspecies factors, the human MTD is expected to lie near 0.5–1 mg kg-¹ day-¹ (35–70 mg per 70 kg). The profile below therefore covers that range.

3.1 Functional / clinical effects  
• Hepatic—dominant:  
  – Asymptomatic or mild right-upper-quadrant discomfort.  
  – Transient hepatomegaly.  
  – Increased serum ALT, AST, γ-GT, ALP, total bilirubin.  
• Portal circulation: sub-clinical portal hypertension possible with early sinusoidal endothelial injury; splenomegaly unlikely within 28 days.  
• GI tract lumen: no appreciable motility change anticipated; bolus volatilisation may induce transient eructation or nausea.  
• Pancreas / gall bladder: no evidence of direct toxicity in sub-chronic studies.  

3.2 Histopathology (predicted from animal data)  
• Liver (central target)  
  – Centrilobular (zone 3) hepatocyte hypertrophy and occasional single-cell necrosis.  
  – Glycogen depletion in centrilobular hepatocytes.  
  – Mild to moderate bile-duct hyperplasia.  
  – Minimal perisinusoidal fibrosis (reversible at this stage).  
  – Endothelial cell swelling in central veins/sinusoids – harbinger of hemangiosarcoma observed in longer studies.  
• Stomach / intestine  
  – Possibly focal superficial epithelial degeneration in the forestomach of rodents; homologous human tissue (oesophagus) may experience trivial irritation; not expected to be histologically significant or persistent after 28 days.

3.3 Clinical pathology  
• Serum chemistry: ↑ALT, ↑AST, ↑ALP, ↑γGT, mild ↑total bilirubin, ↓albumin/globulin ratio (adaptive).  
• Haematology: marginal thrombocytopenia may occur secondary to early portal changes.  
• Urine: Elevated thiodiacetic acid and 2-chloro-2-hydroxyacetic acid—useful biomarkers of exposure.

4. Mechanistic explanation (toxicodynamics)  
Step 1 – Absorption and first-pass extraction deliver high VC concentrations to hepatocytes and endothelial cells.  
Step 2 – CYP2E1 converts VC to CEO and CAA.  
Step 3 – CEO forms DNA adducts (e.g., 7-(2-oxoethyl)-guanine) and protein adducts, triggering:  
  a) Oxidative stress (↑ROS, GSH depletion) → reversible hepatocyte necrosis/apoptosis.  
  b) Activation of stellate cells → extracellular matrix deposition (early fibrosis).  
  c) Endothelial DNA damage → cell proliferation and, with longer exposure, hemangiosarcoma initiation.  
Step 4 – Kupffer-cell activation and cytokine release (TNF-α, IL-6) amplify bile-duct proliferation and mild cholestasis (↑ALP, bilirubin).  

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Hepatocyte hypertrophy & enzyme induction – adaptive; reversible within weeks after cessation; monitorable by serum ALT/AST and liver ultrasound.  
• Mild single-cell necrosis, endothelial swelling – adverse (initiation of pathology); largely reversible in the 28-day frame; monitorable by serum enzymes and, if needed, transient elastography or MRI.  
• Early bile-duct hyperplasia & perisinusoidal fibrosis – adverse; low-grade fibrosis generally reversible if exposure stops; monitorable by γ-GT, ALP and non-invasive imaging.  
• Portal hypertension endpoints (splenomegaly, thrombocytopenia) – borderline adverse; potentially reversible; monitorable via platelet counts and Doppler ultrasound.  
• Gastro-mucosal irritation – adaptive and fully reversible; monitorable via symptom reporting.  

6. Toxicodynamic activity classification (digestive system)  
• Liver: HIGH  
• Other GI tissues (stomach, intestine): LOW  
Systemic availability (oral) to liver: HIGH

7. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No controlled human oral studies – reliance on animal data and inhalation epidemiology.  
• Lack of 28-day rodent study at true MTD; existing sub-chronic studies span lower dose ranges.  
• High volatility complicates accurate dose metrics in gavage studies; real internal dose may vary.  
• Possible synergy with alcohol (induces CYP2E1) not considered in this isolated assessment.

8. Overall conclusion  
At a daily human oral MTD administered for 28 days, vinyl chloride is predicted to produce primarily hepatic (digestive-system) effects—centrilobular hypertrophy, mild necrosis, early bile-duct proliferation, and biochemistry indicative of cholestasis. These changes are mechanistically linked to CYP2E1-mediated formation of reactive metabolites and are expected to be mostly reversible upon cessation, although they are considered adverse because they represent structural and functional liver injury. Direct toxicity to the remainder of the gastrointestinal tract is minimal, largely limited to transient irritation due to the substance’s volatility. Continuous clinical monitoring of liver enzymes and imaging provides suitable follow-up; early discontinuation should prevent progression to chronic fibrosis or tumorigenesis seen in longer-term exposures.

Key references  
• ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride. 2006.  
• IARC Monographs Vol. 100F. Vinyl chloride. 2012.  
• ECHA. Registration Dossier Vinyl chloride. 2023.  
• Feron VJ et al. Subchronic oral toxicity of vinyl chloride in rats. Food Chem Toxicol 1983.  
• Til HP et al. Oral carcinogenicity study of vinyl chloride in rats. Toxicology 1991.  
• Reitz RH et al. Pharmacokinetics of vinyl chloride in humans and animals. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1996.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of repeated-dose oral exposure to  
6PPQ (6PPD-quinone, CAS 2754428-18-5)

1. Substance identity and context  
• 6PPQ is the oxidation product of the tyre-antioxidant 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8).  
• Structure: 1,4-benzoquinone bearing two anilide substituents.  The molecule contains:  
  – A redox-active para-benzo-quinone ring (Michael acceptor)  
  – Two aniline-type secondary amines (potential met-hemoglobin formers)  
• Physico-chemical data (predicted / measured)  
  MW ≈ 298 g mol⁻¹; log Kow ≈ 3.9–4.4; water solubility ≈ 0.2–0.5 mg L⁻¹; pKa (amine) ≈ 4.5–5.0.

2. Data availability  
• No guideline 28-day oral study in any mammalian species is available for 6PPQ (data gap).  
• Information sources considered  
  – Parent 6PPD 28-day and 90-day rat studies (Union Carbide, 1972; ECHA REACH dossier).  
  – In-silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6; Derek Nexus v.6.2).  
  – In-vitro data on 6PPQ: oxidative stress in human HepG2 (Tian et al., 2023); GSH depletion in Caco-2 (unpublished preprint, 2022).  
  – Aquatic toxicity work showing extreme fish lethality (Tian, 2021; Kolodziej, 2022) – used qualitatively for mode-of-action (quinone reactivity).  
  – General toxicology of aniline/quinone diamines.

Weight-of-evidence conclusions therefore rely heavily on read-across from:  
• 6PPD (closest structural analogue; high-quality rat data).  
• Classical p-phenylenediamine quinones (e.g., DMBQ) for mechanistic insight.

3. Systemic availability after oral dosing  
• Absorption: Moderate (30–60 %) expected.  LogKow ~4 promotes passive diffusion; pKa indicates partial protonation in stomach but unionised in intestine.  
• First-pass metabolism: High.  Conjugation with GSH, glucuronide/sulfate of resulting hydroquinone predicted.  
• Plasma binding: high (~95 %) due to lipophilicity.  
Classification – Systemic availability: MEDIUM

4. Expected digestive-system findings at a human Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) given daily for 28 days

4.1 Functional/clinical effects  
• Acute local irritation – epigastric burning, nausea, occasional vomiting and diarrhoea beginning within first few days; caused by redox cycling and protein adduct formation in gastric/duodenal mucosa.  
• Mild methemoglobinemia (5–15 %), leading to non-specific symptoms (fatigue, mild cyanosis) and possible secondary gastrointestinal discomfort (hypoxic nausea).  
• Hepatic enzyme induction: asymptomatic but detectable ↑ ALT/AST (≤2–3-fold), ↑ ALP, and ↑ bilirubin in a minority of individuals.  
• Transient abdominal pain after meals (observed with structural analogues containing aniline groups).

4.2 Pathology

A. Gastro-intestinal tract  
• Stomach: multifocal superficial erosions; minimal to mild inflammatory infiltrate (neutrophils).  
• Small intestine: shortening of villi, mild enterocyte vacuolation, depletion of intracellular GSH (shown in Caco-2 model, ICₕ₀ ~15 µM).  
• Large intestine: generally unremarkable; occasional focal congestion.

B. Liver (part of digestive system accessorily)  
• Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (enzyme-induction pattern).  
• Occasional single-cell necrosis; rare pigment-laden Kupffer cells.  
• Increased relative liver weight (~10–15 %, rat 6PPD data at 300 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹).  
• No fibrosis after 28 days.

C. Gall bladder / bile ducts  
• No primary lesions anticipated; possible functional cholestasis reflected by ↑ serum bile acids.

4.3 Clinical pathology  
• Mild normocytic anaemia (Hb ↓ 5–10 %; read-across from 6PPD 90-day rat study).  
• Methemoglobin 5–15 % of total Hb.  
• Slight ↓ RBC glutathione.  
• ↑ ALT, AST, ALP, GGT; total bilirubin borderline high.  
• Faecal occult blood positive in a minority of subjects with gastric erosions.

5. Mechanistic explanation (how & why)

Step 1: Chemical reactivity  
• Quinone ring is a Michael acceptor → covalent binding to cysteine residues on mucosal proteins.  
• Reaction depletes local GSH, leading to oxidative stress (ROS) and mitochondrial dysfunction (HepG2 IC₅₀ for ROS 6 µM; Tian et al., 2023).

Step 2: Oxidative injury in GI mucosa  
• ROS and protein adducts cause lipid peroxidation → membrane damage → erosions/ulcers.  
• Adaptive up-regulation of NRF2 target genes (HO-1, NQO1) demonstrated in Caco-2 (2–3-fold, 6 h).

Step 3: Systemic consequences  
• Absorbed aniline groups undergo hepatic N-oxidation → nitroso metabolites that oxidise haem iron → methemoglobin (classical for p-phenylenediamines).  
• Hepatocellular hypertrophy is adaptive CYP1A/2B induction secondary to redox stress; mild necrosis reflects threshold exceedance of antioxidant defence.

6. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability

Effect                                        | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinical monitoring
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Gastric/duodenal erosions                   | ADVERSE          | Yes (1–2 wk) | Endoscopy, faecal occult blood
Nausea/vomiting/diarrhoea                   | ADVERSE          | Rapid (<48 h)| Symptom observation, electrolytes
Redox-driven NRF2 enzyme induction          | ADAPTIVE         | Yes          | Not routinely monitored
Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy    | ADAPTIVE (dose-dependent) | Yes (weeks) | Serum ALT/AST, ultrasound liver size
Single-cell hepatocellular necrosis         | ADVERSE (mild)   | Yes          | ALT, AST, GLDH
Methemoglobinemia ≤15 %                     | ADVERSE if symptomatic | Yes (hours) | Co-oximetry
Mild anaemia                                | ADVERSE (borderline) | Yes (weeks) | CBC, reticulocytes

7. Toxicodynamic activity classification  
Given the strong electrophilic (quinone) centre and positive in-silico alerts for protein binding and oxidative stress, overall toxicodynamic potency toward GI tissues is judged HIGH.

8. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Absence of any mammalian repeat-dose study on 6PPQ itself.  
• Human MTD not empirically established—extrapolation from rat 6PPD NOAEL (50 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹) plus reactive-site adjustment factor.  
• Potential microbiome effects entirely unknown.  
• No clinical reports of intentional or accidental human ingestion.  
The predictions should therefore be regarded as provisional and conservative.

9. Overall conclusion  
At a daily human MTD administered for 28 days, 6PPQ is predicted to cause primarily local oxidative/irritant damage to the gastric and upper intestinal mucosa and mild, reversible redox-driven liver changes.  All effects are mechanistically linked to the quinone’s electrophilicity and the aniline-mediated formation of methemoglobin.  Most lesions are expected to be reversible within days to weeks once exposure ceases and can be clinically monitored through standard gastroenterological and clinical-chemistry parameters.
